title: Aion Core — Domain Model and Persistence Contract
intention: Define the vocabulary every other part of Aion speaks: the event types, identifiers, workflow status, error taxonomy, and the persistence contract. When the engine records an activity completion, a client lists running workflows, a store persists history, or a worker reports a result, they all use the types defined here. The types must make illegal states unrepresentable — a completed workflow cannot also be running, an activity result cannot exist without a preceding schedule. Nothing here depends on beamr, the engine, networking, or any storage backend. This is the still point at the centre: pure types and one trait, so the rest of Aion can be built against stable contracts.
problem: A durable workflow engine is an event-sourcing system; the authoritative state of every workflow is a sequence of events, and if those events are modelled inconsistently across the engine, store, wire protocol, and replay logic, the system rots from the inside.

Many independent consumers must agree exactly on what an event is: the engine produces them, store backends persist them, the replay engine reconstructs state from them, the WebSocket layer streams them, clients query summaries derived from them, and workers produce results that become them.

The engine must not know which backend stores events; it must talk to storage through one trait that external crates (Meridian's storage layer, third parties) can implement without pulling in the engine.
This must be settled before any engine or SDK code exists, because everything downstream references it.

solution.summary: Two leaf crates with no Aion dependencies of their own. aion-core holds the pure domain model: Event enum with lifecycle/activity/timer/signal/child variants, newtype identifiers (WorkflowId, ActivityId, TimerId, RunId), WorkflowStatus as a projection over event history, WorkflowFilter/WorkflowSummary query types, and the ActivityError/WorkflowError taxonomy with a retryable/terminal split. aion-store holds the EventStore trait (append with expected-sequence guard, read_history, list_active, query, schedule_timer, expired_timers), the StoreError type, and InMemoryStore — the reference implementation every durable backend must match. Events carry serialised Payload values, not generic type parameters, so a single Event type flows through store, wire, and replay without monomorphisation.

decisions.title: Two leaf crates: types vs trait
decisions.choice: aion-core (domain types) is separate from aion-store (EventStore trait + in-memory impl). Clients and workers need the types but must never link the store trait. Keeping them separate keeps the dependency graph honest. Both depend on no other Aion crate.

decisions.title: Type-erased payloads, not generic events
decisions.choice: Activity inputs/results, signal payloads, and workflow inputs are stored as an opaque Payload newtype (bytes + content-type tag), not as a generic Event<T>. The engine and store are type-erased; only the SDK knows concrete Gleam types. Rejected Event<T>: it would force a type parameter through the entire engine and store, and a store cannot be generic over every workflow's payload types at once.

decisions.title: Event envelope carries sequence + timestamp + workflow id
decisions.choice: Every event has a monotonic per-workflow sequence number, a recorded timestamp, and its workflow id. The sequence is the basis for optimistic-concurrency append. The recorded timestamp is the determinism source for workflow.now — never the wall clock — which is why it lives on every event's envelope.

decisions.title: Identifiers are distinct newtypes
decisions.choice: WorkflowId, ActivityId, TimerId, RunId are distinct newtypes (mostly over UUID) so the compiler rejects passing one where another is expected. ActivityId derives from the scheduling sequence position so replay can match a recorded result to its call deterministically. RunId distinguishes successive runs of the same logical workflow (reset / continue-as-new).

decisions.title: Status is a projection, never a stored mutable field
decisions.choice: WorkflowStatus (Running, Completed, Failed, Cancelled, TimedOut) is derived from the last lifecycle event in history, not stored independently. A backend may cache it for query performance, but events remain authoritative. No code path may set status independently of the events that justify it.

decisions.title: One EventStore trait, timers included
decisions.choice: Timers live on the same trait as events (schedule_timer, expired_timers) rather than a separate TimerStore, because scheduling a timer is itself an event and the two are written in one logical transaction. Rejected separate EventStore/TimerStore/VisibilityStore traits: premature decomposition that fractures atomic-append and complicates the common single-backend case.

decisions.title: Engine supplies expected sequence; store does not invent it
decisions.choice: append takes expected_seq and fails with SequenceConflict if the stored head differs. The store is a dumb, correct ledger; sequencing authority lives in the engine where the single writer per workflow lives. Rejected store-assigned sequences: they hide write conflicts the engine must see to preserve replay integrity.

decisions.title: In-memory store is the behavioural oracle
decisions.choice: aion-store ships InMemoryStore, a correct non-durable EventStore. It is the reference: the behaviour every durable backend must match, used as the oracle in cross-backend equivalence tests and as the store for the engine's own tests (so engine tests need no database).

decisions.title: Retryable/terminal modelled in the type
decisions.choice: ActivityError carries an explicit retryable-vs-terminal classification rather than a string or bool. The engine consults it to decide whether a failed activity is retried per policy or fails the workflow. Modelling it in the type makes the contract explicit at every boundary it crosses.

decisions.title: StoreError taxonomy
decisions.choice: StoreError is a closed enum: SequenceConflict, NotFound, Backend(impl error), Serialization. Backends map their internal failures into these so the engine handles a uniform error surface regardless of backend.

constraints.description: lib.rs/mod.rs are declarations and re-exports only.
aion-core and aion-store are leaf crates — they depend on no other Aion crate. Structural; must hold.
Every type that crosses the wire or the store derives Serialize + Deserialize (these are data types, not credentials).
WorkflowStatus is always derivable from event history; no independent status mutation.
InMemoryStore must pass the same behavioural test suite future durable backends are held to — it is the oracle, so it must be correct.
Identifiers are newtypes; no bare Uuid or String in public signatures where an ID is meant.

goals: Event enum covers the full workflow/activity/timer/signal/child lifecycle, each variant with a sequence+timestamp+workflow-id envelope.
Payload newtype carries opaque serialised values with a content-type tag; round-trips losslessly.
Newtype identifiers (WorkflowId, ActivityId, TimerId, RunId) with compiler-enforced distinctness.
WorkflowStatus projects deterministically from event history.
WorkflowFilter + WorkflowSummary support listing by type, status, time range, and parent.
EventStore trait: append (with expected_seq guard), read_history, list_active, query, schedule_timer, expired_timers.
StoreError closed taxonomy with SequenceConflict, NotFound, Backend, Serialization.
InMemoryStore passes a behavioural test suite that serves as the cross-backend oracle.
non_goals: No durable storage backend (libSQL is AS, PostgreSQL is AX).
No replay logic (AD) — this defines the events replay consumes, not replay itself.
No wire/gRPC protocol (aion-proto in AW) — types derive serde; gRPC mapping is separate.
No engine behaviour — no lifecycle management or scheduling (AE).
No event compaction or snapshotting (later concern once history size is a measured problem).

structure.crates/aion-store/src/memory.rs: [AC-006, AC-007] InMemoryStore reference impl + behavioural test suite
